phrasal_verbs.meaning: say something suddenly and without thinking about the effect it will have, usually because you are nervous or excited
become available to buy or see; become easy to notice; become known; be spoken, heard, or understood in a particular way
say something suddenly, usually something that surprises or shocks people
stop talking because you have forgotten what you were going to say
if news gets (a)round, a lot of people hear it
be connected to a place by telephone; make someone understand what you are trying to say
talk about something that is intended to be a secret
give someone something, for example a message, that someone else has given you
explain an idea, belief, etc in a way that is easy to understand
write something on a piece of paper so that it will not be forgotten and can be looked at later; state officially how something should be done
make it difficult to hear what someone says by shouting while they are speaking
state your opinion firmly and publicly about something, especially in order to protest against or defend something
discuss a problem or a plan
succeed in persuading someone to agree something; discuss something in a general way and without dealing with the most important issues

argument: have / get into an argument (with sb)
ask: ask a lot / too much (of sb)
notice: at short / a moment's / a few hours' notice
paper: not worth the paper it's written/printed on
pen: the pen is mightier than the sword
talk: talk your way into / out of (doing)
word: put in a (good) word for sb
write: have sth / be written all over your face

F.title: Phrasal verbs with down
F.instructions: Tick the phrasal verbs which are also connected to the idea of 'defeating someone or being defeated'.

questions.sentence: Rose backed down when she realised she was wrong.
With my arthritis, it's actually quite difficult to bend down.
I almost broke down in tears when I heard the news.
If the vote goes the wrong way, it could bring down the government.
The council's planning to clamp down on youths drinking in the streets.
The Minister was forced to climb down in the face of so much evidence.

I'll just jot a few notes down while we talk.
Gareth really let me down when he told Andrea what I'd told him.
Dave didn't want to get married at first, but I've managed to wear him down!

N.title: Word formation (Rhetoric text)
N.instructions: Use the word given in capitals to form a word that fits in the space.
N.passage: The (1) ______ 'never judge a book by its cover' could not be more true for *Ridiculous Rules*. The cover is blank, whereas the book is crammed full of examples. Allen is an (2) ______ critic of much of what is taught, and has issued a (3) ______ of war against textbooks which tell lies. Take the ridiculous and (4) ______ rule of never ending a sentence with a preposition. The story goes that Churchill, well known for his numerous (5) ______ as well as for being Prime Minister, received a manuscript back from an ignorant (6) ______, who told him he had to (7) ______ a sentence. Churchill responded by making the forceful (8) ______ in the margin: 'This is an impertinence up with which I will not put.' – the (9) ______ being that not to end a sentence with a preposition often sounds ridiculous. Sadly, Allen informs us that the story is probably mere (10) ______, and that Churchill may have actually only written 'rubbish!'.

questions.sentence: Allen is an (2) ...... (SPEAK) critic
...has issued a (3) ...... (DECLARE) of war
Take the ridiculous and (4) ...... (MEAN) rule
Well known for his numerous (5) ...... (WRITE)
Received a manuscript back from an ignorant (6) ...... (EDIT)
Who told him he had to (7) ...... (PHRASE) a sentence
Churchill responded by making the forceful (8) ...... (STATE) in the margin
The (9) ...... (IMPLY) being that not to end a sentence with a preposition sounds ridiculous
The story is probably mere (10) ...... (HEAR)
